title: Model Challengers
text:
The production forecast should not change just because a model sounds more advanced. This page compares challenger models against the current regression stack before any promotion into the public prediction cards.
Current challengers:

Stepwise OLS: an interpretable regression that selects terms by AIC.
Count models: Poisson, quasi-Poisson, negative binomial, and two-stage zero-aware goal models.
Smooth models: a GAM Poisson model that can learn curved relationships.
Result classifiers: ordinal logistic, multinomial logistic, regularized multinomial, classification tree, and random forest result models.
Expanded population sweep: rolling form, opponent form, head-to-head history, rest, experience, tournament context, GBM, random forest, SVM, and shrinkage baselines.
XGBoost goals: a gradient-boosted count model when the local xgboost package is installed.
Random forest goals: a free local tree fallback while XGBoost is not installed.

How to read the tables:

RMSE is the average size of the goal prediction error. Lower is better.
MAE is the average absolute goal error. Lower is better.
Brier score measures probability quality for win, draw, and loss forecasts. Lower is better.
Log loss heavily penalizes confident wrong probabilities. Lower is better.
Accuracy is the share of matches where the top predicted result matched the actual result. Higher is better.
Time split is the stricter validation check because newer matches are held out from older training data.

3 Expanded Goal Model Suite
This section compares goal-count models on the same historical modeling frame. These are candidate engines for projected scores and expected goals.

if (nrow(expanded_goals) > 0 && requireNamespace("ggplot2", quietly = TRUE)) {
  goal_plot <- expanded_goals |>
    dplyr::filter(status == "fit") |>
    dplyr::mutate(
      validation_type = validation_label(validation_type),
      model = reorder(model, test_rmse)
    )

  ggplot2::ggplot(goal_plot, ggplot2::aes(x = test_rmse, y = model)) +
    ggplot2::geom_col(fill = "#0f6b68", width = 0.66) +
    ggplot2::geom_text(
      ggplot2::aes(label = round(test_rmse, 3)),
      hjust = -0.08,
      color = "#24313d",
      size = 3.4
    ) +
    ggplot2::facet_wrap(~ validation_type, scales = "free_y") +
    ggplot2::coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, max(goal_plot$test_rmse, na.rm = TRUE) * 1.14)) +
    ggplot2::labs(
      title = "Expanded goal model comparison",
      x = "RMSE",
      y = NULL
    ) +
    ggplot2::theme_minimal(base_size = 12)
} else {
  plot.new()
  text(0.5, 0.5, "Expanded goal RMSE plot is available after the model suite is refreshed.")
}





Goal-model RMSE by validation split. Lower is better.

5 Expanded Population Feature Sweep
This sweep uses a wider local R feature population: historical rows, rolling team form, opponent form, head-to-head history, rest spacing, team experience, and tournament context. These are not automatically promoted. They first need calibration checks, knockout handling review, and repeatable backtesting.

title: Model Challengers
text:
6 RMSE Visual


Show code
if (nrow(challengers) > 0 && requireNamespace("ggplot2", quietly = TRUE)) {
  plot_data <- challengers |>
    dplyr::arrange(test_rmse) |>
    dplyr::mutate(model = factor(model, levels = rev(model)))

  ggplot2::ggplot(plot_data, ggplot2::aes(x = test_rmse, y = model, fill = model_family)) +
    ggplot2::geom_col(width = 0.64, show.legend = FALSE) +
    ggplot2::geom_text(
      ggplot2::aes(label = round(test_rmse, 3)),
      hjust = -0.12,
      color = "#24313d",
      size = 3.7
    ) +
    ggplot2::scale_fill_manual(values = c(
      "Interpretable regression" = "#0f6b68",
      "Gradient-boosted trees" = "#d9a441",
      "Tree ensemble fallback" = "#3d5873"
    )) +
    ggplot2::coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, max(plot_data$test_rmse, na.rm = TRUE) * 1.14)) +
    ggplot2::labs(
      title = "Goal model challenger leaderboard",
      x = "RMSE",
      y = NULL

title: Model Challengers
text:
10 Interpretation
The challenger models are useful for two separate reasons:

Stepwise regression helps simplify the equation while staying interpretable.
Tree models can capture nonlinear interactions without hand-writing every term.
Count models better match the fact that goals are non-negative integers.
Smooth models can improve accuracy when the relationship between team strength and goals is curved rather than straight.
Multinomial and ordinal models give direct win, draw, and loss probabilities.

The tree results should be treated as a candidate signal, not an automatic upgrade. Before promotion, the next pass should compare calibration, tournament backtesting, knockout advancement behavior, and stability across multiple time splits.

title: Distributions
text:
1 Purpose
Each variable has an explicit statistical shape. Soccer data mixes low-count outcomes, ordered categorical results, probabilities, text/news counts, player availability flags, and continuous context variables.
The workflow lists plausible distributions, builds diagnostics, and filters models that do not fit the data or the betting question.

title: Distributions
text:
4 Candidate Distributions


Goals scoredPoisson, negative binomial, zero-inflated count models, bivariate Poisson, Skellam goal difference.Goals are low nonnegative counts.


Match resultOrdinal logistic, multinomial logistic, adjacent-category models, paired-score simulation.Useful for win/draw/loss odds comparison.


Player and news countsPoisson or negative binomial with exposure offsets where needed.Useful for goals, mentions, injuries, cards, and appearances.


ProbabilitiesBeta, Dirichlet, calibration curves, Brier/log-loss summaries.Useful once predictions are compared with market-implied probabilities.


Continuous contextNormal, t, lognormal, gamma, empirical splines, or transformed variables.Useful for weather, travel, rest, Elo differences, and player minutes.


Latent team strengthGaussian random effects, dynamic state-space ratings, hierarchical priors.Useful when teams have uneven schedule strength and sparse recent games.




Show code

    display_table(caption = "How each distribution is used in the modeling workflow.")

  display_table(empty_note("Distribution mapping is available after running R/24_distribution_diagnostics.R."))
}



How each distribution is used in the modeling workflow.


Distribution
Soccer Target
Current Model Use




Bernoulli
Single yes/no event: win, scored at least once, clean sheet, both teams scored.
Active for binary side targets and feature engineering.


Binomial
Number of successes across a fixed set of matches, such as wins in a team window.
Used for window summaries and team-form checks.


Poisson
Team goals, cards, injuries, news mentions, and other event counts.
Active for goals and event-count challengers.


Geometric
Number of matches until the next scoring success or clean sheet.
Diagnostic candidate for scoring droughts.


Uniform
Random simulation draws and randomized train/test assignment.
Active in simulation and validation sampling, not as an observed match model.


Normal
Large-sample residual checks for linear models and continuous context features.
Diagnostic baseline; not assumed for raw goals.


Exponential
Time until the next match or next event when the event rate is roughly constant.
Diagnostic candidate for rest-time spacing.


Gamma
Positive waiting times and skewed continuous context, such as rest days.
Diagnostic candidate for rest-time spacing and skewed measurements.


Beta
Model probabilities and calibration uncertainty for win/draw/loss forecasts.
Active for probability calibration summaries.


t
Heavy-tailed residuals when normal errors understate outliers.
Candidate residual model when tails are heavier than normal.


Chi-squared
Goodness-of-fit, independence tests, and residual variance checks.
Active in diagnostics.


F
Variance comparisons across competitions, eras, or model residual groups.
Active in diagnostics.

Autocorrelation means model errors are related across nearby rows in time. If errors stay positive or negative for a stretch, the model may be missing a time pattern such as team form, schedule congestion, or changing tournament style.

title: Distributions
text:
7 Modeling Implications
The current diagnostics point to these practical choices:

Use Poisson and negative binomial families for goals, cards, injuries, and news-count features because these targets are event counts.
Keep Bernoulli, binomial, and beta thinking around win flags, scoring flags, form-window summaries, and probability calibration.
Use gamma, exponential, and geometric ideas for rest, event timing, and scoring drought features, but only after they improve validation metrics.
Use normal, t, chi-squared, and F distributions mainly for diagnostics: residual shape, autocorrelation, heteroskedasticity, goodness of fit, and variance shifts.
Use uniform draws for Monte Carlo simulation and randomized validation, not as a claim about match outcomes.

title: Distributions
text:
8 Review Notes Captured
The public materials reviewed emphasize a few habits that fit this project well:

Use computing to build intuition, not just final answers.
Put distribution assumptions beside visuals so model choices are auditable.
Compare models on held-out or cross-validated performance, not training fit alone.
Teach the reader the data grain before showing the model.
Keep source code reproducible while making the public page readable.

Sources reviewed include Professor Justin Post’s personal/course site, his ST 558 course repository, his Shiny teaching app page, and the Distributome project linked from those teaching resources.
Reference links:

Justin B. Post personal site
ST 558 Data Science for Statisticians

9 Model Quality Checklist

Goals distribution diagnostics by tournament era and confederation.
Mean-versus-variance checks for count features.
Calibration plots for ordinal result probabilities.
Cross-validation by time split.
Predicted versus observed W/D/L rates by probability bucket.
Residual autocorrelation checks after every major model refresh.
Distribution-specific tests before promoting a challenger model.

Methodology

How The Forecast Works


The site combines historical international results, team-strength features, recent form, venue context, weather, and live football feeds where available. The public forecast is an ensemble of a result model and a score-grid model. In knockout matches, the draw-after-regulation probability is converted into advance probability because the match must produce a team that moves on.




Result probabilitiesEstimates regulation outcome probabilities from team strength and context, then converts knockout ties into advance probabilities.


Score forecastUses a Poisson goals model to project expected goals and likely scorelines.


Similarity checkCompares fixtures with similar historical matches as a challenger model.



This is a forecasting and research project. It does not guarantee accuracy, and it should not be treated as financial advice.

title: Predictions
text:
Reading The Forecast

Win probability is the model-estimated chance that a specific team wins the match in regulation or group-stage scoring.
Advance probability appears for knockout matches. It includes the path through regulation, extra time, or penalties.
Level after regulation is the chance that a knockout match is tied after 90 minutes. It is not treated as a final draw.
Draw probability applies to matches where a draw can be the final result.
Prediction strength is the top outcome probability grouped into plain language. It is not a guarantee.
Model agreement counts how many baseline models point to the same winner, draw, or advancing team.
Expected goals is the model average score, not the single most likely score.
Most likely score is the exact scoreline with the largest probability in the score grid.
Over 2.5 goals means the match has at least 3 total goals.
Both teams to score means each team scores at least one goal.


Forecasts are probabilistic and should be used for research, discussion, and model review. They are not financial advice or guaranteed betting edges.

This report checks whether the first regression models are behaving well enough to interpret.
The checks focus on three practical questions:

Are the predictors repeating the same information?
Do residuals show patterns the model missed?
Is a count model a better shape for goals than simple linear regression?

4 Collinearity
Collinearity means two or more predictors are carrying nearly the same signal. It does not always ruin predictions, but it can make individual coefficients unstable.

text:
6 Poisson Checks
Poisson regression is a natural model for goals because goals are counts. The main issue is overdispersion.
Overdispersion means the observed goal counts vary more than a basic Poisson model expects. If this is large, the next goals model should consider a negative-binomial or paired-goal approach.

title: Regression Diagnostics
text:
7 Interpretation
These diagnostics are the gate before model comparison:

If VIF is low, coefficient directions are easier to trust.
If Durbin-Watson is close to 2, residuals are not strongly sequential in the ordered rows.
If heteroskedasticity appears, OLS standard errors should be treated carefully.
If Poisson overdispersion appears, expected-goal predictions may still be useful, but uncertainty intervals need a wider model.

title: Goals Forecast

The Poisson goals model predicts how many goals a team is expected to score.
This is a better statistical shape for goals than simple linear regression because goals are counts: 0, 1, 2, 3, and so on. A Poisson model keeps the expected value positive and models scoring as a count process.

title: OLS Benchmark
text:
This report documents the goals model for the World Cup prediction project.
The target is team goals scored in a historical international match:
\[
Y_{i,m} = \text{goals scored by team } i \text{ in match } m
\]
Goals are non-negative counts. The distribution report compares count-model shapes used for soccer scoring.

title: OLS Benchmark
text:
4 Elo Feature
For one team against one opponent, the expected result before kickoff is:
\[
p =
\frac{1}{1 + 10^{-\left(\text{Team Elo} + \text{Home Adv.} - \text{Opponent Elo}\right)/400}}
\]
Where:

Team Elo is the team’s pre-match rating.
Opponent Elo is the opponent’s pre-match rating.
Home Adv. is the home advantage adjustment.
p is the expected result on a 0 to 1 scale.

7 Interpretation
The signs match soccer intuition:

pre_elo is positive: stronger teams score more.
opponent_pre_elo is negative: stronger opponents suppress scoring.
pre_match_expected_result is positive: teams expected to perform better score more.
listed_homeTRUE is positive: listed home teams score more.

title: Win / Draw / Loss Model

The ordered outcome is:
\[
Y_{i,m}^{ord} \in \{\text{loss}, \text{draw}, \text{win}\}
\]
with:
\[
\text{loss} < \text{draw} < \text{win}
\]
This output supports betting-market comparison because it estimates match outcome probabilities.

title: Model Overview
text:
This report scores the 2026 World Cup fixture list with the current local models.
Each fixture receives:

OLS expected goals for the home and away team.
Poisson expected goals for the home and away team.
Ordinal win, draw, and loss probabilities from the home team’s perspective.
Knockout-stage advance probabilities, where draw-after-regulation is reallocated through a team-strength tiebreak signal.
Weather context when venue-level weather summaries are available.

3 Prediction Table
The table below shows a compact version of the scored fixture set. Probabilities are from the home-team perspective.


Show code
predictions <- read_csv_if_exists(file.path(root, "data", "processed", "modeling", "world_cup_2026_fixture_predictions.csv"))
if (nrow(predictions) > 0 && !"is_knockout_match" %in% names(predictions)) {
  predictions <- predictions |>
    dplyr::mutate(
      is_knockout_match = as.Date(date) >= as.Date("2026-06-28"),
      regulation_draw_prob = pred_draw_prob,
      home_advance_prob = pred_home_win_prob,
      away_advance_prob = pred_away_win_prob,
      predicted_advancing_team = NA_character_
    )
}

if (nrow(predictions) > 0) {
  predictions |>
    dplyr::mutate(
      score = dplyr::case_when(
        !is.na(home_score) & !is.na(away_score) ~ paste0(home_score, "-", away_score),
        TRUE ~ ""
      ),
      poisson_expected_goals = paste0(
        fmt_number(pred_home_goals_poisson, 2),
        " - ",
        fmt_number(pred_away_goals_poisson, 2)
      ),
      ols_expected_goals = paste0(
        fmt_number(pred_home_goals_ols, 2),
        " - ",
        fmt_number(pred_away_goals_ols, 2)
      ),
      home_win = fmt_percent(pred_home_win_prob, 1),
      draw_or_level = dplyr::if_else(
        is_knockout_match,
        paste0("Level after regulation ", fmt_percent(regulation_draw_prob, 1)),
        paste0("Draw ", fmt_percent(pred_draw_prob, 1))
      ),
      away_win = fmt_percent(pred_away_win_prob, 1),
      home_advances = dplyr::if_else(
        is_knockout_match,
        fmt_percent(home_advance_prob, 1),
        ""
      ),
      away_advances = dplyr::if_else(
        is_knockout_match,
        fmt_percent(away_advance_prob, 1),
        ""
      ),
      public_pick = dplyr::if_else(
        is_knockout_match,
        predicted_advancing_team,
        predicted_result
      ),
      phase = dplyr::if_else(is_knockout_match, "Knockout", "Group"),
      weather = dplyr::if_else(
        is.na(avg_temperature_2m),
        "",
        paste0(fmt_number(avg_temperature_2m, 1), " C, wind ", fmt_number(avg_wind_speed_10m, 1))
      )
    ) |>
    dplyr::select(
      date,
      phase,
      home_team,
      away_team,
      score,
      poisson_expected_goals,
      ols_expected_goals,
      home_win,
      draw_or_level,
      away_win,
      home_advances,
      away_advances,
      public_pick,
      city,
      weather
    ) |>
    display_table(caption = "Scored 2026 fixtures.", max_rows = 24)
} else {

title: Model Overview
Model Overview
text:
6 Interpretation
The fixture table is the model’s operational output:

The Poisson goal estimates are the main expected-goals baseline.
The ordinal model supplies win/draw/loss probabilities.
Knockout forecasts convert draw-after-regulation into advance probabilities, because those matches must eventually produce a winner.
OLS remains a transparent benchmark, but count models are better suited for goals.
Weather is shown as context until enough historical weather coverage exists for coefficient estimation.

title: Multi-Sport Model Expansion
text:
4 Reference Implementation
Basketball is the recommended first non-soccer reference implementation. It has a clean team-versus-team event structure, no draw outcome in most competitions, and enough overlap with the World Cup workflow to prove the reusable ingestion, validation, modeling, and reporting layers.
The first production pass for each sport will use the same sequence:

Ingest raw API snapshots with timestamped manifests.
Build cleaned game, team, player, standings, odds, and statistics tables where available.
Create model-ready rows with one row per team-game or event participant.
Run validation tests before modeling.
Fit baseline models first, then Bayesian models where the sport has enough historical depth.
Backtest by time, compare calibration, and publish small result samples.

title: Multi-Sport Model Expansion
text:
5 Current Lineage Rules

Raw JSON stays local in data/raw/apisports_inventory/.
Sanitized summaries are written to data/processed/multisport/.
Published pages show samples, endpoint status, timestamps, and field definitions.
The public site shows compact summaries only; full raw feeds remain in local project storage.

title: Similar Match Model
text:
The KNN model is a similarity model. For a team in a match, it finds historical team-match rows that look most similar, then summarizes what happened in those nearby games.
This gives the project a useful contrast:

OLS explains average goal changes with coefficients.
Ordinal logistic explains ordered win/draw/loss probabilities.
KNN asks: “What happened when past teams looked like this team in this matchup?”

title: Similar Match Model
text:
The KNN model is a similarity model. For a team in a match, it finds historical team-match rows that look most similar, then summarizes what happened in those nearby games.
This gives the project a useful contrast:

OLS explains average goal changes with coefficients.
Ordinal logistic explains ordered win/draw/loss probabilities.

title: Similar Match Model
text:
2 How It Works
KNN stands for K-Nearest Neighbors.
For each test row, the model:

Standardizes the model inputs so all features are on comparable scales.
Finds the 25 closest historical team-match rows.
Predicts goals by averaging the goals scored by those neighbors.
Predicts loss, draw, and win probabilities by counting neighbor outcomes.

} else {

title: Similar Match Model
text:
5 Interpretation
KNN is easy to explain because the prediction comes from comparable historical cases. That is also its limitation.
The model depends heavily on:

The features used to define similarity.
The number of neighbors.
Whether older matches should count the same as recent matches.
Whether rare outcomes, especially draws, are represented well enough in the neighbor set.

KNN is strongest as a comparison model. If it beats the simpler regression models, the feature set probably contains useful nonlinear patterns. If it loses, the added flexibility is not earning its keep.
